op.name: shares_memory
op.notes: Determine if two arrays share memory.
op.summary: Determine if two arrays share memory.

blocks.text: Determine if two arrays share memory.

This function can be exponentially slow for some inputs, unless
   `max_work` is set to zero or a positive integer.
   If in doubt, use `numpy.may_share_memory` instead.

desc_blocks.text:
Effort to spend on solving the overlap problem (maximum number
of candidate solutions to consider). The following special
values are recognized:

max_work=-1 (default)
    The problem is solved exactly. In this case, the function returns
    True only if there is an element shared between the arrays. Finding
    the exact solution may take extremely long in some cases.
max_work=0
    Only the memory bounds of a and b are checked.
    This is equivalent to using ``may_share_memory()``.

blocks.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> x = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4])
>>> np.shares_memory(x, np.array([5, 6, 7]))
False
>>> np.shares_memory(x[::2], x)
True
>>> np.shares_memory(x[::2], x[1::2])
False

blocks.text:
Checking whether two arrays share memory is NP-complete, and
runtime may increase exponentially in the number of
dimensions. Hence, `max_work` should generally be set to a finite
number, as it is possible to construct examples that take
extremely long to run:

blocks.code:
>>> from numpy.lib.stride_tricks import as_strided
>>> x = np.zeros([192163377], dtype=np.int8)
>>> x1 = as_strided(
...     x, strides=(36674, 61119, 85569), shape=(1049, 1049, 1049))
>>> x2 = as_strided(
...     x[64023025:], strides=(12223, 12224, 1), shape=(1049, 1049, 1))
>>> np.shares_memory(x1, x2, max_work=1000)
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
numpy.exceptions.TooHardError: Exceeded max_work

blocks.text:
Running ``np.shares_memory(x1, x2)`` without `max_work` set takes
around 1 minute for this case. It is possible to find problems
that take still significantly longer.
